coredata.dc:title: Combination of fluorescence spectroscopy and electrochemical technique as a novel and sensitive electro-optical detection system for gaseous and dissolved oxygen detection using nitrogen-doped quantum dots through the amperomric reduction of oxygen
coredata.dc:creator: Doroodmand, Mohammad Mahdi
Askari, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description:
A novel dissolved and gaseous oxygen electro-optical sensor was fabricated based on the dependency of the fluorescence intensity of a synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDots) during the amperometric reduction of O2 at potential of −0.55V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for about 20s using a three-electrode system including gold rod as worker electrode, platinum rod as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (3.0molL−1 Cl−) as reference electrode, followed by measuring the fluorescence intensity at excitation wavelength of 350nm. Under optimized condition, i.e. 5.0μgmL−1 of N-CDots, ionic strength of 0.5molL−1 and room temperature, the linear range for the dissolved oxygen was estimated to between 0.11 and 22μgmL−1 with detection limit of 0.07μgmL−1 (n=4). For gaseous oxygen the linear dynamic range was between 3.0 and 48.0% with the limit of detection of 0.95% (n=4). The response time (t
90 ) of the sensor was estimated to be 20s. No interfering effect was observed during analysis of at least 200-fold excess (vs. 2.0μgmL−1 dissolved oxygen) of organic and inorganic species such as, K2SO4, KNO3, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaClO4, NaF, NH4Cl, CaCl2 and Na3PO4, except NO2 − and HCO3 − which interfered for respectively 12-fold and 75-fold excess. The reliability of the method was also evaluated via analyses of waste and industrial samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Dissolved oxygen
Gaseous oxygen
Electro-optic
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of temperature and quenching mechanisms of C-Dot's fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRSEM (A) and HRTEM (B, C) images of the synthesized C-Dots.
XPS survey spectrum (A), and high-resolution spectrum of C1s (B), N1s (C), O1s (D).
Typical absorption, excitation and normalized fluorescence spectra of the synthesized C-Dots at 10.0nm band slit.
Schematics of A) the designed system during fluorescence measuring the DO and B) the designed system during fluorescence measuring the gaseous oxygen.
Cyclic voltammograms of A) the fabricated electrode system and B) the commercial electrode during analysis of Fe(CN)6 3− with 1.0×10−3 M concentration at scan rate of 100mVs−1.

Effect of A) initial pH on the fluorescence intensity of 10.0ppm of DO at potential of −0.55V, time duration of 20s (n=4) and temperature on the fluorescent intensity of C-Dots during reduction of 8.0ppm DO at −0.55V potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) at time duration of 20.0s (n=4).
Effect of temperature on quenching of fluorescence by Q at pH=5.0 (n=4).

A) Differential pulse voltammetry of three different concentrations of DO (3.0, 6.0 and 8.0ppm) at scan rate of 100mVs−1 and B) cyclic voltammogram of 6.0ppm DO at pH5.0, and room temperature at scan rate of 100mVs−1.
Diagram of signal vs. reduction time during amperometric reduction of 15.0ppm of DO at −0.55V (vs. Ag/AgCl).
Calibration curve. Inset: first five points of the calibration curve at optimum condition.
Interferring effect of different coexisting ions during detection of DO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Commercial DO meter as ref. amperometery.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between some selected methods with the developed methods. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Units ppm (mgL−1) and % are related to the DO aqueous media.

head.ce:title: Combination of fluorescence spectroscopy and electrochemical technique as a novel and sensitive electro-optical detection system for gaseous and dissolved oxygen detection using nitrogen-doped quantum dots through the amperomric reduction of oxygen

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel dissolved and gaseous oxygen electro-optical sensor was fabricated based on the dependency of the fluorescence intensity of a synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDots) during the amperometric reduction of O2 at potential of −0.55V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for about 20s using a three-electrode system including gold rod as worker electrode, platinum rod as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (3.0molL−1 Cl−) as reference electrode, followed by measuring the fluorescence intensity at excitation wavelength of 350nm. Under optimized condition, i.e. 5.0μgmL−1 of N-CDots, ionic strength of 0.5molL−1 and room temperature, the linear range for the dissolved oxygen was estimated to between 0.11 and 22μgmL−1 with detection limit of 0.07μgmL−1 (n=4). For gaseous oxygen the linear dynamic range was between 3.0 and 48.0% with the limit of detection of 0.95% (n=4). The response time (t
90 ) of the sensor was estimated to be 20s. No interfering effect was observed during analysis of at least 200-fold excess (vs. 2.0μgmL−1 dissolved oxygen) of organic and inorganic species such as, K2SO4, KNO3, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaClO4, NaF, NH4Cl, CaCl2 and Na3PO4, except NO2 − and HCO3 − which interfered for respectively 12-fold and 75-fold excess. The reliability of the method was also evaluated via analyses of waste and industrial samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-Dots) are an important class of fluorescent nanoparticles that hold great promise in bio-imaging and sensing applications. C-Dots generally consist of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, have quasi-spherical shape (<10nm) and display multicolor emission [1]. C-Dots as a class of ‘zero-dimensional’ carbon nanomaterials have attracted intensive attention for a variety of potential applications in optoelectronic devices, biological labeling and bio-imaging, due to their alluring optical properties, excellent biocompatibility as well as low toxicity. Particularly, considerable research effort has been focused on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of C-Dots [2]. The fluorescence intensity of nanostructures depends on different factors such as pH, ionic strength, temperature, kind and concentration of quencher, type and viscosity of solvent. For instance about the C-Dots, the pH of the solution may be considered as one of the important factors having significantly influence on their fluorescence properties. This property may be selected a good probe when combination with chemical or electrochemical processes which change the pH during their reaction. About the oxygen, change in the acidity of the solution during its electrochemical reduction, majorly affects the fluorescence intensity of C-Dots. Therefore, this method may be considered as selective probe for ultra-sensitive oxygen detection during combination of fluorescence spectroscopy and electrochemical technique.

Molecular oxygen is considered as one of the most important chemical species in our environment since it is found as either a reactant or a product in a large number of chemical and biochemical reactions. Determination of the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) in a solution is necessary in the medical, food processing, waste management, and petrochemical industries [3]. DO in the surface water is used by all forms of aquatic life and it participates in a variety of chemical, biological and biochemical processes. DO concentration is an indicator to monitor water quality [4]. This is because many chemical and biological reactions in water depend directly or indirectly on the amount of available oxygen. Oxygen monitoring is of high importance in the fermentation bioprocess, because it affects metabolism in a multitude of ways [5]. The DO measurement is therefore a cornerstone in natural water science.

Due to its participation in several important biogeochemical reactions, the O2 molecule is often selected as the indicator used to examine the rate of primary production in the upper ocean, organic matter oxidation in the aphotic water column, and sediments. It serves as a unique water mass identifier in ocean circulation and climate change studies [6]. Also, aquatic life depends on DO concentration; for example, between 5 and 7mgL−1 is acceptable for warm water fish, and from 7 to 33.8mgL−1 is very good for most stream fishes. When the level in the water is below ~5mgL−1, it might be harm aquatic life. On the other hand, a high DO level in a community water supply is good because it makes drinking water taste better. However, high DO levels speed up corrosion in the water pipes. For this reason, it is necessary to monitor the DO in water [7].

There are two ways to prepare DO calibration (standardization) solutions with known concentrations: (1) saturating water with air at a fixed temperature and air pressure using known saturated concentrations and (2) preparing a DO solution using some primary DO measurement method [8]. Even small changes in the DO content can have serious consequences for many marine organisms, as DO concentration influences the cycling of nitrogen and other redox-sensitive elements. Decrease in the DO concentration leads to the formation of hypoxic regions in coastal seas, in sediments, or in the open ocean, which are inhabitable for most marine organisms. DO concentration is related to the changes in the ocean circulation and to the uptake of CO2 by the ocean. Unfortunately, all of these changes in turn have relationship to the climate change [8].

Accurate measurements of DO concentration are very important for studying these processes, understanding their role and predicting climate changes. These processes are spread over the entire vast area of the world's oceans and at the same time are slow and need to be monitored over long periods of time. The performance of oxygen sensors based on amperometric and (especially) optical probes has dramatically improved in recent years. The latter requirement means that, the sensors have to be rigorously calibrated so that the measured results are traceable to the SI (International System of Units). Consequently, the sensors need to be calibrated with solutions of accurately known oxygen concentration in order to be reliable from aspects like sensor drift, temperature, salinity and pressure influences. Oxygen is an unstable analyte thus it significantly complicates the sensor's calibration [8].

The first method for determination of DO is based on titrimetry which was introduced by Winkler [9]. The O2 in the water quantitatively oxidizes iodide to iodine, which is determined by titration with standardized thiosulfate solution. This method requires relatively large sample volumes (∼60mL), and also is time consumption. Some others extended Winkler method by little changes in procedure, which are classified in titration methods but the effort for the introduction of more reliable methods is needed [9]. The widely studied method for DO measurement is the “Clark” electrode and its modified forms, including the potentiometric DO sensors based on metal oxides electrodes. Although electrochemical-based DO sensors can provide accurate measuring results [10,11], its drawbacks such as slow response time, a “static” reading and the aging of the electrodes [4] have limited its general application. Optical methods such as fiber optic sensors offer attractive alternatives to the electrochemical-based DO sensors due to the fast response time, little oxygen consumption and no interference from exterior electromagnetic fields [4]. Among these optical methods for DO detection, fluorescence-based and luminescence-based DO sensors have received special attentions. Such sensors exploit a decrease in fluorescence intensity of an excited indicator as a result of the quenching effect induced by the energy-transfer mechanism with oxygen molecule [4].

The gaseous O2 sensors are widely used as monitoring and control devices in various applications in the industry, i.e. automobile and metallurgical industries, environmental and bio-logical control processes [12]. Often conductivity characteristic of the material has been exploited for use as an O2 gas sensor [13]. Other methods for detection of O2 gas are based of blowing the gas in to the aqueous solution and determine the DO [14]. But significant disadvantageous of the resistive O2 gas sensors is the long response time to changes in oxygen partial pressure [13]. To solve these problems hereby in the present work, it is focused on a new method based on a new kind of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodot (N-CDots) as fluorescent agent for detection and determination of gaseous and DO oxygen at μgmL−1 level via combination of a three-electrode system and fluorescent spectrometry.

All the chemicals were from their analytical grade. Triply-distilled water was used in the present work. Potassium chloride (KCl), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and hydrogen chloride (HCl) were purchased from Merck. Analytical grade of gases such as N2 (purity: 99.98%, company) and O2 (purity: 99.98%, company) were prepared. The three-electrode included a gold rod (diameter: 0.8mm) as a worker electrode, a 0.8-mm platinum rod as a counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl (3.0molL−1 Cl−) electrode as a reference electrode. In this study, mgL−1 unit was selected for the concentrations of DO and C-Cots and parts per million (ppm) for the gaseous O2 solution.

Pyrolysis process was done using a domestic microwave (LG-WaveDom (MS2387RS)). All the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrofluorometer, equipped with a thermos-stated cell compartment containing 1.0-cm quartz cuvettes. The excitation wavelengths were selected using the synthesized C-Dots. Different methods were applied for characterization of synthesized C-Dots, including flourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (FT-IR-8300 Shimadzo), patterned X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8Advance with Cu-Kα, λ=0.1542nm), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Zeiss-EM10C-80KV, 300kV), and high resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM, KYKY-EM3500). All the XRD characterizations and the calculations were performed using “X′ PERT HIGH SCORE” software. A domestic microwave oven (model: Samsung, power: 1200W) equipped with temperature programming with ±2°C precision was adopted to synthesized carbon nanostructures. A pH meter (Metrohm 827pHlab Swiss made) was used for accurate adjustment the pH data. Also water bath (model: Polystat cc2) was used to control the temperature during the study. A DO meter (lutron electronic DO-5510) was utilized as reference probe for the DO and gaseous oxygen detection. Electroanalyzer (μoutolab) was adopted for the DO reduction by amperometry.

To synthesize N-CDots, briefly 25.0g citric acid (15.6mmole) was dissolved in to 400.0mL triply-distilled water. After that 5.0mL triethylenetetraamine (32.6mmole) was dropped into the solution during ~3.0min time intervals, followed by assisting with microwave radiation (power=650W) for ~50min time duration. The synthesized N-CDots did not need any passivation or activation. High resolution scanning/transmission electron microscopic (HRSEM and HRTEM) image of the synthesized C-Dots have been shown in Fig. 1(A) and (B and C). Both HRSEM and HRTEM photographs showed size distribution between 7 and 11nm. More detail of the characterization of the synthesized N-doped C-Dots has been evaluated in detail in our previous report [15].

The surface composition and elemental analysis for the resultant dots were characterized by XPS technique. The three peaks at 292.3, 407.4 and 539.1eV shown in the XPS spectrum of these dots (Fig. 2(A) can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The XPS results indicate that these dots are mainly composed of C, N, and O element. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2(B)) shows two peaks at 291.2, and 293.4eV, which are attributed to, CN, and CO, respectively. The one peak at 405.0 in N1s spectrum (Fig. 2(C)) are attributed to NH groups; while the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2(D)) shows one peaks at 537.6eV, which are attributed to the CO and/or COC bands. The above observations confirm that the synthesized C-Dots functioned with amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties which would origin from precursors. The typical absorption, excitation and normalized fluorescence spectra of the synthesized C-Dots at 10.0nm excitation and emission band slit have been shown in Fig. 3 . As shown maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at 350nm wavelength.

The fluorescent N-CDots were used as fluorophore probe. For this purpose, a solution of C-Dots in 0.5molL−1 KCl was prepared. Then the pH was set to 5.0. To reach this aim, HCl and NaOH solution were adopted for adjusting the pH value. The fluorescence gradient was measured via difference between the fluorescence intensities of the solution before and after applying the reduction process based on the recommended procedure. During the reduction process, the solution was stirred with ~300rpm by a stirrer through a small magnet situated in the cell. The fluorescent cuvette was the cell and a three-thin electrode system was introduced to the cell. Finally, the quenching value was correlated to the concentration of DO in the sample solution. In addition, an electroanalyzer was adopted for DO reduction by amperometry at potential of −0.55V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 20s time duration. Standard dissolved and gaseous oxygen solution was prepared by blowing N2 and O2 via controlling by a reference probe. The PL spectra were also obtained twice by the spectrofluorometer before and after the electrochemical reduction process. The schematic of the designed system is shown in Fig. 4 .A.

Briefly the system for DO stock solution consisted of two O2 and N2 cylinders. The concentration of DO was controlled by regulating the cylinders via detection by standard DO meter in a C-Dots solution as shown in Fig. 4.A. After fixing the DO concentration during standardization by the reference DO meter, 2.0mL of solution was introduced to spectrofluorometer to record the fluorescence spectra. Then the fresh solution was introduced to electroanalyzer to reduce the DO. After that the reduced solution was again introduced to spectrofluorometer to record the fluorescence spectra for the second time. Finally, the difference between the fluorescence intensities during quenching process was related to concentration of DO.

For gaseous oxygen determination, the schematic of the designed system was shown in Fig. 4.B. The system for gaseous oxygen was just like that for the DO setup, except the initial step at which initially the percentage of O2 was controlled by the pressure regulator's valve equipped on the O2 and N2 gas cylinders, followed by standardization using the standard O2 meter inside a tree-necked bottle (volume: ~100mL). After fixing the O2 percentage in an adequate value, it was purged into the C-Dots solution for maximum 2.0min. to prepare the standard O2 gaseous solution. Then the other step was followed just similar to the procedure recommended for the DO detection.

To fabricate the reference electrode, a silver rod (Diameter: 1.2mm, length: 15.0cm purity: 99.9%) was oxidized via contacting with nitric acid solution (purity, 60% w/w, 12.95M) for several seconds (~15s) and then immersed into a saturated KCl for ~5min, followed by washing with 100mL triply-distilled water. This process led to immobile KCl (sat'd) on the surface of the Ag rod as electrode. In addition, gold and platinum rods (purity: 99.99%) with 0.8mm diameter were selected as working and counter electrodes. The geometry surface area of the working electrode (gold rod) was estimated to be 0.738±0.001cm2.

For analyzing the real samples, each real sample was analyzed using correlation equation of the standard curve. For this purpose, different DO standard solutions were initially prepared according to the recommended procedure, followed by standardization by a Clark commercial DO meter.

During adoption of nanostructures such as N-CDot as marker kits for direct analysis of any analyst, it is needed to standardize it. But unfortunately there are few reference or standard materials for standardization of nanostructures especially when having very large active surface area. At this condition, during using nanostructures such as the synthesized N-CDots as selective probe for the detection analytical purposes, it is recommended to i) optimize the effective parameters to reach the highest sensitivity and reproducibility, ii) analyze the real samples by standard addition method, and ii) evaluate the reliability of the method by some statistical tests. In this study, detail of the optimization process, figures of merit and some statistical evaluations are as follows:

To evaluate the reliability as well as the applicability of the fabricated electrode system, Fe(CN)6 3− (1.0mmolL−1) was selected as selected probe. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was also selected as the electrochemical mode. The voltammograms have been shown in Fig. 5 . Based on the voltammograms, no significant difference was observed during comparison between the CV of the fabricated electrode system and the commercial electrodes (Metrohm).

To reach the highest sensitivity for detection of gaseous and DO oxygen, some parameters such as pH, temperature, reduction time, applied potential and CD concentration were optimized. One-at-a time method was selected for the optimization process. Due to the similarity between detection process of both gaseous and dissolved oxygen, therefore the optimization process was evaluated for a DO standard solution. This optimization was therefore interpreted for detection of both gaseous and dissolved oxygen. Detail of these optimizations is as follows.

The initial pH value was considered as one of the most important parameters during the electrochemical reduction of DO. For this purpose, HCl and NaOH solution was adopted. The results are shown in Fig. 6 .A. As shown maximum sensitivity has been shown at pH5.0. In another word maximum quantity of DO has been reduced during applying electrical potential of −0.55V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at time duration of 20s. Therefore, this pH value was selected for the reduction process.

No significant change was also observed on the O2 solubility at different pH values between 3 and 12 during determination of the DO using the Clack DO meter as reference probe. At high acidic and basic condition ~5% reduction was observed in the DO concentration probably due to the salting-out effect. However, for most of the real sample solutions, the matrix of the tested sample is partially the same as the initial one. Also about the real samples with high acidic and high basic pH, the fluorescence intensity is so high that operation of the analysis of the unoptimized condition of the pH would not provide any significant problem.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and quenching mechanisms of C-Dot's fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7 shows the effect of temperature on the quenching process of the C-Dots fluorescence intensity during interaction with a quencher (Q) (i.e. H2O2 as product of reduction of DO). In this figure, the F C-Dots was defined as the initial fluorescence of the C-Dots in the absence of Q, whereas F
Q was the intensity of the quenched fluorescence of C-Dots in the presence of the Q species. As shown (Fig. 7), the fluorescence intensities in quenching processes showed a positive slope as temperature increases. Generally, to determine the fluorescence restoring or quenching mechanisms, the measurements must be performed at different temperatures. It was known that, the mechanism would be dynamic if the slope of fluorescence variation versus temperature was positive; otherwise, the mechanism was static [16]. Based on the positive slopes observed for the correlated lines in Fig. 7, it was concluded that quenching fluorescence process followed a dynamic process. Dynamic quenching or collisional quenching was therefore occurred when the excited state fluorophores (i.e. C-Dots) was deactivated by other molecules (i.e. Q), in the solution. The Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) can be used for this case [16]. Where K
SV was Stern-Volmer quenching constant indicating the sensitivity of the fluorophore to a quencher. The F
0 was the bare fluorescence in the absence of quencher and F was the quenched fluorescence of C-Dots. The [Q] was the concentration of the quencher [16]. Based on the dependency of the fluorescence intensity to the temperature, it was concluded that the fluorescence of the synthesized C-Dots obeyed from the dynamic mechanism.
(1)
F 0
/
F
C ‐ Dots
= 1 +
K SV
Q

The DO has been analyzed during electrochemical reduction of the DO to H2O2. About the DO detection and determination, as the sensitivity (cathodic peak current) is low, therefore fluorescence intensity has been selected as the detection of sensitive and ultra-trace detection of DO. In this study by spiking H2O2 standard solutions with different concentrations, it was possible to confirm i) H2O2 as both the quencher as well as the product of the electrochemical reduction and ii) the probable mechanism related to the operation of the synthesized C-Dots. The quench behavior of the DO has been directly correlated to the H2O2 as the electrochemical product of O2 during the electrochemical process. Consequently, there was an indirect relationship between the quench of the fluorescence intensity and the DO. Also the gaseous O2, can be simply detected via purging O2 and N2 into a N-CDots-containing solution, resulting to have a saturated aqueous solution and determination according to the procedure explained in detail for the DO.

To further study the effect of temperature, in a constant concentration of DO at six different temperature's detections was evaluated in detail. By increasing the temperature, the fluorescence gradient got higher sensitivity (Fig. 6.B). As shown in spite of high sensitivity of the fluorescent gradients, but the fluorescence difference was not significant. Consequently, room temperature was selected as the working detection for simplicity.

Based on all the reported results any species that is able to change the pH of the solution can simply affect the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized C-Dots. For instance, oxidation of formic acid to the CO2 can be detected at the ultra-trace levels by fluorescence detection system. Consequently, the selectivity of the system can be controlled based on the applied potential to the electrode system and simultaneously the ultra-trace detection system is based on the fluorescence property of the synthesized C-Dots. The possibility for the occurrence of this oxidation depends on different electrochemical factors, such as kind of the working electrode, the initial pH and ionic strength, applied potential, catalyst(s), polarization, etc. In this study, about this electrode system no interfering effect was expected for formic acid during detection of oxygen, probably due to the presence of significant difference between the electrochemical half potential of O2/O2 2− and formic acid/CO2. In another word, O2 and formic acid were reduced at two significant potentials (vs. the Ag/AgCl). Consequently, these reagents can be detected independently in the presence of each other.

As it has been shown in Eq. (2), the product of electro-reduction of oxygen in acid was H2O2, which involved the charge transfer of two electrons to give peroxide [17]. The reduction of O2 to H2O2 was occurred at −0.38V potential (vs. Ag/AgCl), which was accepted by both differential pulse voltammetry (Fig. 8 .A) and CV technique (Fig. 8.B). Based on the results, not only the CV (Fig. 8.B), but also a more sensitive electrochemical technique such as differential pulse voltammetry (Fig. 8.A) pointed to the electrical potential of −0.38V for reduction of DO. The importance of the combination of fluorescence probe and electrochemical process is future confirmed when considering the selectivity of the probe during the operation of the redox reaction at a distinct applied potential, high sensitivity (fluorescence intensity) of the trace and ultra-trace concentrations of the analyte, and the improved detection limit of the fluorescence spectroscopy, compared to the electrochemical techniques. However potential of −0.38V (vs. Ag/AgCl) showed maximum cathodic peak current during the reduction process, but to get maximum electrochemical current density, −0.55V potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) was selected for the reduction of DO with amperometry. At this condition the solution was also stirred for having both diffusion and convection-controlled mass transfer process during having more efficient and more rapid the bulk electro-analytical process. This effect led to have the highest sensitivity during electrochemical process.
(2)
O 2
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→ 2
H 2
O 2

As expected, the higher time duration of the applied potential by the amperometry mode, the more amounts of DO were reduced on the surface of the electrode system. The results are shown in Fig. 9 at time duration between 5 and 45s. The reduction of DO to H2O2 was accrued for ~20s time interval at the constant potential of −0.55V (vs. Ag/AgCl).

Based on the results (Fig. 9), more intense signal was detected by increasing the time duration of the reduction. The increase in the reduction time therefore led to the increase in the H2O2 concentration as quencher, which resulted in the decrease of the fluorescence signal. Consequently, the fluorescence gradient (ΔF) was promoted by increasing the reduction time interval. However, the analyzing time may increase and the signal would be overloaded for high values of the DO. So a reduction time of the 20s was chosen as optimum. Never the less, for special purpose, about the DO solutions with concentrations <~110.0ngmL−1, it was possible to use reduction times such as 40.0s or even higher.

To optimize the quantity of fluorophore, the synthesized C-Dots were purified initially via washing with acetone (~10mL) for at least 3 times, followed by sonication inside a sonicator bath at about 2.0 KHz frequency at 60°C temperature for about 10min. The acetone was evaporated inside vacuum furnace at ~50°C temperature. Based on the thermo-gravimetric analysis, these conditions led to have C-Dots with maximum purity (>99%) Then, 1000mL stock aqueous solution of C-Dots (100.0mgL−1) was prepared via weighting the purified C-Dots. The stock solution was then sonicated using a solicitor at frequency around 5MHz for about 10min. to provide the homogenized solution. Due to the small nanometric size of the synthesized C-Dots, these nanostructures were simply distributed homogeny all over the solution. Afterward different fresh concentrations of C-Dots at a range between 1.0 and 25.0mgL−1 were prepared during evaluated during analysis of DO (10.0ppm) at time duration of 20.0s. But as there was no significant difference between 5.0, 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0mgL−1 of the C-Dots, therefore 5.0mgL−1C-Dots was selected as optimum value.

In this study, due to the importance of the solution volume in the bulk analysis of DO, a fluorescence 1.0-cm quartz cell was selected for using as the electrochemical reaction cell as well as the fluorescence spectroscopic cell. Because of the limitation of the cell volume, it was aimed to fabricate a reference Ag/AgCl electrode. For this purpose, simply an Ag wire was immersed inside a dilute nitric acid solution (~1.0mmolL−1) for ~1.0min to oxidize the surface of the Ag wire to Ag+, followed by suddenly inserting inside a situated AgCl suspension to immobilize the Ag wire with AgCl precipitation. For finalizing the fabricating of the reference electrode, concentration of Cl− should also be controlled. At this condition, from one way the concentration of Cl− was important and from the other way the ionic strength of the solution should be optimized. For having reproducible applied potential as well as precise fluorescence intensity specifically during analysis of real samples with different chemical matrices, high ionic strength was selected during using KCl (3.0molL−1). Therefore there was no need to evaluate and optimize the effect of different ionic strength. This selection therefore not only simplified the sample preparation during the DO analysis, but also canceled the need of a commercial Ref. electrode, as well as require to analyze large volume of the sample solution and long time needed for bulk analysis of DO, compared to the analytical conditions needed to be applied using a commercial Ref. electrode.

However according to the salting-out effect, it was expected to have change in the concentration of the DO during adjusting the 3.0molL−1 concentration with KCl solution. But fortunately no significant change was observed (maximum change: <~1%) for the DO concentration during comparing the effect of different ionic strengths on the DO concentration using the DO sensor as reference probe. This was attributed to the trace concentrations of DO inside the aqueous water media, which was not significantly affected by the water/salt electrostatic interactions. Nevertheless for analysis of high concentrations of DO, it was recommended to analyze the DO sample solution rapidly after adjusting the ionic strength or adopting commercial reference electrode.

The calibration curve for DO was illustrated in Fig. 10 . The linear dynamic range was between 0.11 and 22.00mgL−1, with R-squared (R2) value of 0.9952. The sensitivity of this method was evaluated according to the slope (calibration sensitivity) of the calibration curves (Fig. 10). This value was evaluated to 37.33(a.u.)Lmg−1 for DO. Significant difference between the calibration sensitivities was attributed to high and acceptable fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized C-Dots.

The repeatability of this method was also estimated obtained during several analyses of a constant concentration of DO with 12.0mgL−1 concentrations. The RSD% was estimated to be 1.53% (n=5) for DO solution. Based on the 5 replicate analyses, the reproducibility (%RSD) was estimated to be 6.18%, which was acceptable for detection and determination of DO in various real sample media.

The detection limit (DL) was defined as the triple values of the standard deviation of the blank divided by the calibration sensitivity (slope of the calibration curves, Fig. 10). Based on this definition, the limit of detection was found as 0.071ppm (n=5) for DO (Eq. (3)).
(3)
DL = 3
Sb / m = 3 × 0.886 / 37.33 = 0.071
mg
L
− 1

Effect of interferences was also evaluated based on 2.0mgL−1 of DO. For this purpose, some probable coexisting foreign species were selected for evaluation of the selectivity of the proposed method during oxygen detection and determination. In this study, the influence of different fold-excess ratios of these foreign species was individually evaluated, compared to a constant concentration of DO solution. Maximum fold-excess of these species that did not provide any interfering effect has been shown according to the histogram in Fig. 11 . It revealed that, detection of DO reported by this method even in presence of 600.0mgL−1 Ca2+ or 600.0mgL−1 of Mg2+ was simply possible. This result clearly pointed to the selectivity of this method for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of gaseous and dissolved oxygen in the real samples without considering any significant interfering effect.

For more evaluation of the effect of foreign species on the fluorescence intensity during analysis of O2 gas, large concentrations of different gases such as air, CO2, CH4, CO, gasoline vapor, n-hexane, car exhaust and SO2 were qualitatively spiked into an O2-containing gas solution with a fixed molar concentration. Fortunately, no significant interfering effects were observed for any of the tested analysis, showing the selectivity of the introduced method for O2 determination.

The reliability as well as the validity of this method was evaluation via estimation of the relative error percentage during comparison between the results obtained from this method and those estimated based on the Clark commercial DO meter as reference and accepted analytical technique. The results were shown in Table 1 . This test was therefore considered as acceptable analytical technique for approving the reliably and validity of this method for accurate determination of gaseous and DO in the real samples, without having problems such as error of the matrix effect. Consequently, independency from the matrix effect error was considered as another advantageous of this method that canceled the need of standard addition method.

A sensitive and selective method has been introduced for gaseous and DO measurement. The main advantageous of this method is its capability for ultra-trace detection and determination of gaseous and dissolved oxygen in different real samples. Simplicity is also considered as another characteristic of this method. This property is based on the independency of this determination for applying potential and measuring the fluorescence intensity. For bulk analysis of oxygen, it is only needed to apply a fixed potential through the amperomery for a constant time. In Table 2 ; this method is comparable with the previously reported articles from aspects such as detection limit and RSD%. Compared to the previously reported methods, this technique has some beneficial aspects such as more simplicity, high selectivity, fast response time and acceptable detection limit.
The authors wish to acknowledge the support of this work by the Shiraz University Research Council.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mohammad Mahdi Doroodmand Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran. Accociate proffesor in analytical Cheimsitry. Experience: Analytical chemistry, Instrumentation Design, Synthesis of nanostructures, Bioelectrochemistry.
Mohsen Askari Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran. M.S. in analytical Cheimsitry. Experience: Analytical Chemsitry, Detection and determiantion.
